1.9: Diotrephes 'who likes to put himself first' (philoproteuo, v. 9) represents the emergence of local congregational autonomy that resisted the authority of the itinerant apostolic elder. The conflict between the elder's authority (rooted in the apostolic tradition) and Diotrephes's local authority (rooted in congregational control) anticipates the later tension between episcopal and local governance structures. Diotrephes's practices — refusing the elder's letter, refusing to receive traveling brothers, and expelling those who do — constitute a miniature local church coup. 3 John is the NT's only document addressing intra-Christian church politics at this granular level.
